name: London
blurb: The greatest port of the Atlantic world; the Pool of London handles sugar, tobacco and the East India trade.
detail: By 1730 London clears more tonnage than any port in Europe. The Admiralty, Lloyd's coffee-house insurers, and the South Sea and East India Companies all direct world shipping from here. Execution Dock at Wapping is where condemned pirates are hanged at low tide.
population: ~630,000 — the largest city in Europe
defenses: The Tower's batteries; Tilbury and Sheerness forts at the river mouth; the Royal Navy at Chatham and Deptford
trade: Sugar, tobacco, East India calicoes and tea, naval stores, wool cloth out; insurance and credit for half the Atlantic
events: 1701 — Captain Kidd hanged at Execution Dock; the rope breaks, he is strung up again
1711 — South Sea Company founded on dreams of Spanish-American trade
1720 — South Sea Bubble bursts, ruining thousands
1724 — A General History of the Pyrates published, making Blackbeard immortal

name: Bristol
blurb: England's second port and a principal slaving harbour; home town of Blackbeard and Woodes Rogers.
detail: Bristol merchants dominate the Guinea trade in the 1720s–30s, dispatching dozens of slavers a year on the triangular route. Both Edward Teach (Blackbeard) and Woodes Rogers, the privateer who broke pirate Nassau, were Bristol men.
defenses: The narrow tidal Avon gorge is its own defence; Kingroad anchorage in the Severn
trade: Slaves, sugar, tobacco, brass and glass; ~40 Guinea voyages a year at peak
events: 1708 — Woodes Rogers sails with Dampier on the privateering voyage that rescues Selkirk
1717 — Bristol-born Teach takes the name Blackbeard
1730s — overtaken by Liverpool in the slave trade

name: Liverpool
blurb: The fast-rising rival of Bristol: its first wet dock (1715) is the wonder of English engineering.
population: ~12,000 and doubling every generation
defenses: None to speak of — distance from France is its wall
trade: Slaves, Manchester cottons, salt, Irish provisions
events: 1715 — the Old Dock opens, the world's first commercial wet dock
1730 — Liverpool merchants enter the African trade in earnest

name: Amsterdam (Texel)
blurb: Seat of the VOC; the East Indies fleets assemble in the Texel roadstead.
detail: The Dutch East India Company (VOC) is the largest trading corporation on earth, sending ~30 ships a year around the Cape to Batavia. Outward fleets gather at Texel and sail in December–January to catch the season.
defenses: The shoals of the Zuiderzee and the Republic's navy
trade: Spices, tea, porcelain, Baltic grain and timber, world bullion market
events: 1725 — VOC dividend still 25% despite English competition
1730s — ~30 Indiamen a year clear Texel for the Cape

name: Nantes
blurb: France's chief slaving port, fitting out for the Guinea coast and Saint-Domingue.
defenses: The Loire's shifting sands; Château guarding the quays
trade: Slaves, sugar from Saint-Domingue, indiennes (printed calicoes)
events: 1716 — letters patent open the slave trade to French private merchants; Nantes seizes the lead

name: Saint-Malo
blurb: The corsair city: a walled rock of privateers whose captains terrorized English trade in the late wars.
detail: Duguay-Trouin, the greatest of the Malouin corsairs, took Rio de Janeiro itself for ransom in 1711 with a private squadron. In peace the Malouins run interloping voyages to the South Sea and the cod banks.
defenses: Full circuit of ramparts; Fort Royal and tidal reefs all round
trade: Privateering prizes in war; Newfoundland cod, Cádiz silver, South Sea interloping in peace
events: 1711 — Duguay-Trouin storms Rio de Janeiro and ransoms it
1713 — Utrecht peace turns the corsairs back to cod and contraband

name: Lisbon
blurb: Entrepôt of the Brazil trade; the annual fleets bring sugar and Minas Gerais gold.
detail: Brazilian gold pours through Lisbon in the 1720s — the richest regular treasure flow in the Atlantic after the Spanish plate fleets, and a favourite prize for pirates like Bartholomew Roberts, who took the Sagrada Família off Bahia in 1719.
defenses: Belém Tower and the São Julião forts on the Tagus approaches
trade: Brazilian gold and diamonds, sugar, tobacco, Madeira and Port wines, English cloth in
events: 1719 — Roberts takes the Sagrada Família with 40,000 moidores
1729 — diamonds of Minas Gerais reach Lisbon in quantity

name: Cádiz
blurb: Headquarters of the Carrera de Indias since 1717; the flotas for New Spain and Tierra Firme sail from this bay.
detail: The Casa de Contratación moved here from Seville in 1717. Everything legal that Spain ships to or from the Americas — and all registered silver — must pass through Cádiz Bay under convoy.
population: ~30,000 and swelling with the Indies monopoly
defenses: San Sebastián and Santa Catalina castles; the Puntales narrows; a city on an island spit
trade: American silver in; European cloth, wine, mercury and paper out — half of it foreign goods under Spanish names
events: 1717 — Casa de Contratación transferred from Seville
1726 — Hosier's English blockade squadron rots off Portobelo while Cádiz waits for its silver
1730 — the Marqués de Casa Tilly's flota sails on the old rhythm

name: Funchal, Madeira
blurb: First victualling call on almost every outward Atlantic passage; famous for wine that improves at sea.
population: ~5,000 town; ~50,000 on the island
defenses: São Tiago fort and an open roadstead — ships anchor and pray
trade: Madeira wine (the Americas drink it), sugar, ship stores
events: 1720s — Madeira pipe becomes standard sea-stock of every East- and West-Indiaman

name: Santa Cruz de Tenerife
blurb: Canary Islands waypoint where ships pick up the NE trade winds for the Atlantic crossing.
defenses: San Cristóbal castle and shore batteries (they will keep Nelson out in 1797)
trade: Canary wine, orchil dye; last European water before the trades
events: 1706 — Jennings' squadron repulsed in the War of the Succession

name: Praia, Cape Verde
blurb: Last watering stop before the Middle Passage or the long southern run; pirates lurk here for Guinea-bound prizes.
defenses: A small battery on the plateau; mostly the emptiness of the sea
trade: Salt, water, hides, slaves in transit
events: 1721 — Roberts waters in the islands while ravaging the Guinea trade
1722 — the Swallow and Weymouth hunt him through here

name: Salé
blurb: The corsair republic of Bou Regreg; the 'Sallee Rovers' cruise for Christian shipping off Iberia.
detail: Salé's corsairs operated shallow-draught xebecs the European frigates could not follow over the river bar. Though past their 17th-century peak, Sallee Rovers still take prizes and slaves within sight of the European coast in the 1730s.
population: ~25,000 with Rabat across the river
defenses: The river bar (no deep ship can enter), kasbah batteries
trade: Prizes, ransoms, Christian slaves; legitimate hides and wax when piracy is slow
events: 1719 — Defoe's Robinson Crusoe begins his misfortunes as a Sallee slave
1721 — British treaty with Morocco frees 296 captives

name: Algiers
blurb: Greatest of the Barbary corsair states, ransoming captives from all Christendom.
detail: The Regency of Algiers licenses corsair captains (raïs) much as European crowns license privateers. Its galleys and round ships range as far as the Atlantic; European states pay tribute or convoy their Mediterranean trade.
population: ~100,000, of whom thousands are Christian slaves
defenses: The mole and its hundred guns; the kasbah; janissary garrison
trade: Prizes, ransoms and tribute; wheat and wax lawfully
events: 1712 — Dutch buy peace with tribute
1730 — perhaps 2,000 Christian captives await ransom in the bagnios

name: Boston
blurb: Largest town in British America; shipbuilding, cod and the carrying trade.
detail: Boston is the busiest colonial port c.1730 (~13,000 people), launching more ships than the rest of America combined. Its admiralty court hanged the survivors of the Whydah wreck in 1717.
defenses: Castle William on Castle Island; the harbor's maze of islands
trade: Cod to Iberia, rum, ships themselves (sold abroad), molasses in from the islands
events: 1717 — six Whydah survivors hanged; Cotton Mather preaches at them
1723 — gibbet at Nix's Mate displays pirate William Fly's bones (1726)
1730 — ~600 vessels clear annually

name: Newport
blurb: Rhode Island's port has a long, comfortable history with privateers, smugglers and retired pirates.
detail: Newport merchants financed 'Red Sea men' in the 1690s and fenced their plunder. Twenty-six of Charles Harris's pirates were hanged here in one day in July 1723 — the largest mass execution in colonial America.
defenses: Fort Anne (such as it is); Narragansett Bay's deep water
trade: Rum distilled for the Guinea trade, candles, smuggled molasses
events: 1694 — Thomas Tew, hometown hero, dies attacking the Mughal fleet
1723 — 26 of Low's and Harris's men hanged at Gravelly Point in one day

name: New York
blurb: A thriving harbour with a piratical past: Kidd sailed from here, and Madagascar money built fine houses.
detail: In the 1690s New York merchants like Frederick Philipse openly supplied the pirate base at Madagascar. By 1730 the town is respectable — but Kidd's backers' families still own the waterfront.
defenses: Fort George at the Battery; the Narrows
trade: Flour and bread to the sugar islands, furs, logwood, Madeira wine
events: 1696 — Kidd fits out the Adventure Galley at the East River
1698 — Madagascar trade at its height under Governor Fletcher's blind eye
1731 — population passes 10,000

name: Philadelphia
blurb: The Quaker city, fastest-growing port in America, shipping flour and bread to the sugar islands.
population: ~8,000 and growing fastest in America
defenses: Quaker principles — effectively none
trade: Flour, bread, barrel staves to the West Indies; servants and Palatine emigrants in
events: 1723 — a runaway apprentice named Franklin steps ashore
1730s — overtakes Boston in flour exports

name: Hampton Roads
blurb: Gateway of the Chesapeake tobacco fleets; Blackbeard's head was displayed here on a pole in 1718.
detail: The Royal Navy guardships for Virginia anchor here. Lieutenant Maynard returned from Ocracoke in 1718 with Blackbeard's head hanging from his bowsprit; it was set up at the harbour mouth — Blackbeard's Point — as a warning.
population: ~1,500 in Hampton town; the tobacco wealth lives on the plantations
defenses: HM guardships on the Virginia station; Point Comfort battery
trade: Tobacco — the annual fleet of 100+ sail to London and the outports
events: 1718 — Spotswood sets £100 on Blackbeard's head; Maynard collects it
1718 — the head goes up on a pole at Blackbeard's Point

name: Charles Town
blurb: Rice and slaves made Charleston rich — rich enough for Blackbeard to blockade it for medicine in 1718.
detail: In May 1718 Blackbeard's squadron lay off the bar for a week, seizing ships and citizens until the town delivered a chest of medicines. Stede Bonnet, the 'gentleman pirate', was hanged here that December at White Point.
population: ~4,500 (the colony majority enslaved)
defenses: Granville and Craven bastions on the walls; Johnson's Fort on the bar; Sullivan's Island lookout
trade: Rice to Europe, deerskins, naval stores; the largest slave market in North America
events: 1718 — Blackbeard blockades the bar for a medicine chest
1718 — Bonnet and 29 men hanged at White Point in November–December
1719 — colonists overthrow the Proprietors; royal government follows

name: Bath & Ocracoke
blurb: Blackbeard's last lair: a pardon in Bath Town, an anchorage at Ocracoke, and a bloody end in 1718.
detail: Edward Teach took Governor Eden's pardon at Bath in June 1718 and 'retired' to Ocracoke Inlet, fencing goods up the sounds. On 22 November 1718 Lt Robert Maynard's sloops found him at Teach's Hole; Blackbeard died with five pistol balls and twenty sword cuts.
population: Bath ~300; Ocracoke a handful of pilots and wreckers
defenses: The Outer Banks themselves — shoals that eat keels
trade: Naval stores, smuggled sugar; pirate goods sold cheap to grateful planters
events: 1718 (June) — Teach pardoned at Bath, marries his fourteenth wife
1718 (Oct) — pirate revel at Ocracoke with Vane, Rackham and Hornigold's old crews
1718 (22 Nov) — Maynard takes Teach's head at Teach's Hole

name: St. Augustine
blurb: Spain's fortress outpost in Florida, guarding the homeward channel of the plate fleets.
population: ~1,500 souls around the garrison
defenses: Castillo de San Marcos — coquina stone that swallows cannonballs
trade: Situado (the garrison subsidy), oranges, salvage from the wreck coast
events: 1715 — salvage camps for the drowned plate fleet run from here
1728 — Palmer's Carolina raid burns the town but not the castle

name: Bermuda
blurb: Mid-ocean waypoint of the homeward passage; Bermudian sloops are the fastest small craft afloat — pirates' favourite prize.
detail: Nearly every homeward-bound ship from the Caribbean raises Bermuda to fix its position before running east for the Azores. The local cedar sloops, weatherly and quick, were so prized that pirates would swap larger ships for them.
population: ~8,000 on the islands
defenses: The encircling reef — more ships lost to it than to any fleet
trade: Cedar sloops, salt from Turks Islands, carrying trade
events: 1701 — Bermuda sloop design already famous through the islands
1730s — Bermudians rake salt at Turks and trade it coastwise

name: Nassau
blurb: Capital of the Republic of Pirates, 1706–1718. Hornigold, Blackbeard, Vane and Anne Bonny all sailed from this harbour.
detail: After the wars Nassau's wrecked, governorless harbour became the pirate commonwealth: perhaps a thousand sea-rovers under their own rough articles. The harbour was too shallow for warships but perfect for sloops; Hog Island sheltered the careening beach. Woodes Rogers arrived with the royal pardon in July 1718, hanged eight diehards that December, and by 1730 Nassau is nominally lawful — expulsis piratis, restituta commercia.
population: ~1,000 at the pirate peak; ~1,400 settled by 1730
defenses: Fort Nassau (28 guns, chronically ruinous); the shallow bar that keeps frigates out
trade: In the pirate years: everything stolen between Maine and the Main. Now: salvage ('wracking'), salt, turtle, dyewood
events: 1706 — Franco-Spanish raid leaves Nassau governorless; rovers move in
1715 — Jennings brings the plundered Spanish salvage here; the Republic blooms
1717 — perhaps 1,000 pirates use the harbour; the 'Flying Gang' rules
1718 (July) — Woodes Rogers arrives with the King's pardon; Vane fires a fireship and escapes
1718 (Dec) — eight diehards hanged under the fort's walls
1720 — Spanish invasion attempt repulsed by reformed pirates

name: Havana
blurb: The padlock of the Indies: the plate fleets assemble under Morro Castle before running the Florida Strait for Spain.
detail: Every registered peso of American silver funnels through Havana's harbour. Its shipyard builds the largest warships in the Americas, and the guarda costas that prowl for British smugglers (and provoke wars) fit out here.
population: ~50,000 — the third city of the Americas
defenses: El Morro and La Punta at the harbour throat with their chain boom; La Fuerza within; 12-ft walls landward
trade: Silver in transit, tobacco, hides, sugar; the Real Compañía's monopoly from 1740
events: 1715 & 1733 — the wrecked plate fleets' salvage runs from here
1723 — Royal Shipyard launches its first great ships of Cuban cedar
1731 — guarda costa Juan de León Fandiño slices off Captain Jenkins' ear (war follows in '39)

name: Port Royal & Kingston
blurb: Once 'the wickedest city on earth', drowned by the 1692 earthquake; now the Royal Navy's Caribbean base and the pirates' gallows.
detail: Two-thirds of buccaneer Port Royal slid into the harbour on 7 June 1692. By 1730 the navy careens at Port Royal while trade moves to new Kingston across the water. Calico Jack Rackham was hanged on Gallows Point in 1720 and gibbeted on the cay that bears his name; Charles Vane followed in 1721.
population: Port Royal ~1,000; Kingston ~5,000 and rising
defenses: Fort Charles and the Royal Navy's Jamaica squadron; Mosquito Point battery
trade: Sugar (Jamaica now England's richest colony), slaves transshipped, logwood, Spanish contraband via sloops
events: 1692 — earthquake drowns two-thirds of the town, ~2,000 dead
1720 — Rackham, with Bonny and Read aboard, taken drunk at Negril; hanged at Gallows Point
1721 — Vane hanged after shipwreck betrayed him
1722 — hurricane finishes much of what the earthquake left

name: Santiago de Cuba
blurb: Base of the Spanish guarda costas of the Windward Passage — licensed sea-rovers in all but name.
defenses: San Pedro de la Roca castle over a bottleneck entrance
trade: Copper, hides, sugar; condemned 'smuggler' prizes auctioned on the quay
events: 1720s — guarda costas under men like Matthew Luke (an Italian in Spanish service) take English sloops wholesale; Luke is hanged at Jamaica in 1722

name: Tortuga
blurb: The original buccaneer stronghold of the 1630s–80s, under its rock fortress; by 1730 a quiet French settlement, all legend.
detail: Île de la Tortue's Basse-Terre roadstead and Le Vasseur's Fort de Rocher sheltered the brethren of the coast for two generations — l'Ollonais and Henry Morgan recruited here. The buccaneers drifted to Petit-Goâve and then out of history, but every pirate of 1730 grew up on Tortuga tales.
population: A few hundred planters and turtle-hunters
defenses: Fort de Rocher's ruins; the iron-bound north coast
trade: Turtle, hides, a little tobacco — and memory
events: 1640s — Le Vasseur builds the Rock fort and defies Spain
1654 — Spanish take the island (briefly, as always)
1684 — Treaty of Ratisbon begins the end of legal buccaneering

name: Cap-Français
blurb: Booming capital of French Saint-Domingue, the richest sugar colony in the world.
population: ~8,000 town; the plain behind it swallows thousands of the enslaved yearly
defenses: Batteries on the Picolet point; the reef
trade: Sugar and indigo out in fleets; slaves in from Nantes ships
events: 1711 — formally laid out as capital of the north
1730s — Saint-Domingue passes Jamaica as the world's richest colony

name: Petit-Goâve
blurb: The flibustiers' last legal harbour: French buccaneers fitted out here into the 1690s under royal commissions.
detail: After Tortuga faded, Petit-Goâve was where a filibuster could get a French commission, sell a Spanish prize and spend the proceeds. De Graaf and Grammont sailed from here against Veracruz (1683) and Campeche (1685).
defenses: A small fort; the deep cul-de-sac bay
trade: Sugar now; prize goods then
events: 1683 — Veracruz sacked by de Graaf and Grammont from this road
1697 — the Cartagena expedition marks the buccaneers' last muster

name: Léogâne
blurb: Seat of the governors of Saint-Domingue between the buccaneer age and Port-au-Prince's founding.
defenses: Shore batteries on the Cul-de-Sac bay
trade: Sugar, indigo; the colony's law courts and customs
events: 1710s–30s — colonial capital before Port-au-Prince (1749)

name: Santo Domingo
blurb: The oldest European city of the Americas, seat of the audiencia of Hispaniola.
population: ~6,000 — grand buildings, thin purses
defenses: Full circuit of 16th-century walls; Ozama fortress
trade: Hides, cacao, sugar; mostly memories of primacy
events: 1586 — Drake's sack, still the defining trauma
1724 — the Guadalupe and Tolosa survivors are brought here from Samaná

name: San Juan
blurb: Fortress harbour of Puerto Rico; Miguel Enríquez's guarda costas sail from here against interlopers.
defenses: El Morro and San Cristóbal — the strongest works in the eastern Indies
trade: Ginger, hides, contraband condemned by Enríquez's private fleet
events: 1700s–35 — Miguel Enríquez, mulatto shoemaker turned privateer don, runs the most effective guarda costa fleet in the Indies from here

name: Cartagena de Indias
blurb: The treasure citadel of the Spanish Main, terminus of the galeones; the San José blew up off this coast in 1708 with 11 million pesos.
detail: Cartagena's walls and the Boca Chica forts guard the silver of Peru while the galeones load at the Portobelo fair. Pointis and the buccaneers sacked it in 1697 — the last great buccaneering stroke — and Spain rebuilt it stronger than ever.
population: ~12,000 plus garrison
defenses: San Felipe de Barajas hill-castle, full city walls, Boca Chica's gauntlet of forts; Boca Grande sunk with stone
trade: Peru's silver at fair time, slaves under the English asiento, emeralds, pearls from Margarita
events: 1697 — Pointis and the filibusters sack the city
1708 — Wager's Action: the San José explodes off Barú
1728 — galeones system sputtering; fairs irregular
(1741 — Vernon will fail gloriously before these walls)

name: Portobelo
blurb: Site of the famous fair where Peru's silver, carried over the isthmus by mule, meets the galeones from Spain.
detail: For the few weeks of the feria, Portobelo holds more silver per square yard than anywhere on earth; the rest of the year it is a fever-ridden village. Henry Morgan stormed it in 1668; Admiral Vernon would take it again in 1739.
population: ~800 between fairs; ~10,000 during one
defenses: Iron Castle (San Felipe) and San Jerónimo across the bay
trade: The fair: Peruvian silver against European goods, weeks of it
events: 1668 — Morgan storms the Iron Castle
1726 — Hosier's blockade; his crews die of fever by thousands
(1739 — Vernon takes it with six ships)

name: Veracruz
blurb: Atlantic port of New Spain under the island fort of San Juan de Ulúa; terminus of the New Spain flota.
population: ~8,000 (fever keeps it lean)
defenses: San Juan de Ulúa island fortress; ships moor to rings on its walls
trade: Mexican silver, cochineal, vanilla; the flota's European cargoes inland to Mexico City
events: 1683 — the buccaneers' sack under de Graaf and Grammont, never forgotten
1725 — flota under Serrano arrives on the old schedule

name: Campeche
blurb: Logwood port of the Yucatán; the contraband logwood camps nearby bred a generation of Baymen and pirates.
defenses: New walls finished 1704 after one sack too many
trade: Logwood (dyewood) — fought over by Spanish, English cutters and everyone's navies
events: 1685 — Grammont's sack prompts the walls
1716–17 — Spanish sweeps drive English logwood men to piracy; many end at Nassau

name: Bridgetown, Barbados
blurb: First landfall of the English outward passage and the richest sugar island per acre in the empire.
population: ~13,000 town; ~70,000 island, three-quarters enslaved
defenses: Needham's Point fort and a militia of planters; windward position is the true wall
trade: Sugar, rum, molasses out; everything else in
events: 1693 — census already shows the sugar machine complete
1720s — Roberts (who hated Barbadians) flies a flag showing himself standing on a Barbadian's head

name: Saint-Pierre, Martinique
blurb: The 'Paris of the Antilles', commercial capital of the French islands; Roberts burned shipping in its roads in 1720.
defenses: Open roadstead under batteries; the governor at Fort Royal to leeward
trade: Sugar, coffee (new since 1723), Nantes goods
events: 1720 — Roberts burns shipping in the roads; his flag shows a Martinican's skull too
1723 — de Clieu lands the first coffee plant, nursed across the ocean

name: Willemstad, Curaçao
blurb: The great Dutch free port and smuggling hub of the southern Caribbean, trading with everyone Spain forbids.
defenses: Fort Amsterdam at the harbour mouth; neutrality, mostly
trade: Everything, to everyone: slaves, cacao from the forbidden Venezuelan coast, European goods for Spanish silver
events: 1713 — raided for ransom by Cassard's French squadron
1720s — guarda costas and Curaçao sloops fight a quiet permanent war

name: Gorée
blurb: French slaving fort off Cape Verde peninsula, first African call on the Senegambia trade.
population: A few hundred: traders, garrison, captives in transit
defenses: Forts St-François and St-Michel on the islet
trade: Slaves, gum arabic, gold dust, ivory
events: 1677 — taken from the Dutch by d'Estrées; French since
1720s — Compagnie des Indes runs the trade

name: Elmina
blurb: São Jorge da Mina: the oldest European building in the tropics, Dutch headquarters on the Gold Coast.
detail: Built by Portugal in 1482, taken by the Dutch in 1637, Elmina anchors the West India Company's gold and slave trade — three miles from the English at Cape Coast Castle, in eternal commercial war.
population: ~12,000 in the African town beneath the castle
defenses: St George's castle and Fort Coenraadsburg on the hill
trade: Gold dust, slaves, Akan trade networks
events: 1637 — Dutch storm it from the Portuguese
1720 — WIC ships fall to Roberts' squadron cruising the coast

name: Ouidah
blurb: The Slave Coast's busiest roadstead; Roberts took eleven slavers lying here in one day in January 1722.
detail: English, French and Portuguese forts sit side by side at Ouidah, embarking thousands every year. Bartholomew Roberts captured the entire anchorage in January 1722 and burned the Porcupine with slaves aboard — days before HMS Swallow caught him at Cape Lopez.
population: ~20,000 in the African town; the forts a few dozen each
defenses: Three rival European forts two miles inland; the murderous surf is the real gatekeeper
trade: Slaves — perhaps 15,000 a year through the Bight at peak — paid in cowries, cloth, guns
events: 1722 (Jan) — Roberts takes 11 ships in the road, burns the Porcupine
1727 — Dahomey conquers Ouidah from the Hueda kings; trade continues under new masters

name: Cape Coast Castle
blurb: Headquarters of the Royal African Company on the Gold Coast — and the site of the great pirate trial of 1722.
detail: After HMS Swallow destroyed Roberts' squadron, 268 prisoners were tried in the castle hall: 52 hanged in chains along the shore, the largest pirate execution in history. The dungeons below held thousands awaiting the Middle Passage.
population: Castle ~100 Europeans (fever permitting); Fetu town beside it
defenses: 74 guns seaward; the surf; the Royal Navy's Africa station
trade: Gold, slaves, ivory for the RAC — losing ground to separate traders yearly
events: 1722 (Mar–Apr) — trial of Roberts' 268 men; 52 hanged, 37 to the mines, the gibbets visible from sea
1730 — RAC monopoly effectively dead; ten-percenters swarm the coast

name: Luanda
blurb: São Paulo de Loanda: the single greatest slaving port on earth, feeding Brazil for two centuries.
population: ~6,000 free; thousands more in the barracoons
defenses: São Miguel fortress on the height
trade: Slaves to Brazil (~10,000 a year), paid in Brazilian rum and Asian cloth
events: 1641–48 — Dutch interlude, ended by a Brazilian fleet
1720s — Luanda–Rio axis at full horrible efficiency

name: Cape Town
blurb: The Tavern of the Seas: every Indiaman, honest or otherwise, must water under Table Mountain.
detail: The VOC's refreshment station is the hinge of the East. Outward fleets rest here ~3 weeks, then run the roaring forties east. Even pirates on the Madagascar round watered at False Bay, posing as honest traders.
population: ~3,000 in town plus Company servants and the enslaved
defenses: The Castle of Good Hope's five bastions; Table Bay's anchors-dragging northwesters
trade: Refreshment: cattle, wine, wheat, water — sold to every flag at VOC prices
events: 1713 — smallpox from a ship's laundry devastates the Khoikhoi
1722 & 1728 — winter gales wreck whole fleets in Table Bay
1730s — Constantia wine already famous in Europe

name: Saint-Denis, Île Bourbon
blurb: French coffee island where many Madagascar pirates took the 1718 amnesty and settled down as planters.
detail: Bourbon (Réunion) was the pirates' retirement option: La Buse himself took the pardon here — then relapsed, took the Nossa Senhora do Cabo in 1721, and was hanged on this island in 1730, leaving his famous cryptogram of buried treasure.
population: ~8,000, half enslaved, a sprinkling of pardoned pirates
defenses: No harbour at all — an open roadstead and luck
trade: Coffee for the Compagnie des Indes, grown partly with pirate capital
events: 1718 — amnesty brings pirate settlers and their chests
1721 — La Buse takes the Cabo's diamonds at Saint-Denis' own roadstead (the prize lay at Saint-Paul)
1730 (7 July) — La Buse hanged at Saint-Paul, tossing his cryptogram to the crowd: 'find my treasure who can!'

name: Port Louis, Île de France
blurb: Mauritius under new French management since 1721; previously a Dutch isle and a pirate watering stop.
population: A few hundred — chosen chief port in 1731, still mostly plans
defenses: The reef passes; works begun under La Bourdonnais only from 1735
trade: Way-station ambitions; ebony, provisions
events: 1710 — Dutch abandon Mauritius to the rats and the pirates
1721 — France claims it as Île de France
1735 — La Bourdonnais arrives; the real harbour begins

name: Île Sainte-Marie
blurb: The pirate island of the Indian Ocean: Kidd, Avery, Condent and England all careened in its hidden harbour off Madagascar.
detail: Sainte-Marie's reef-locked harbour, friendly Betsimisaraka allies and position athwart the Red Sea and Mocha routes made it the 'pirate round' base from the 1690s. Adam Baldridge ran the trading post fencing plunder to New York. Kidd's Adventure Galley rots on its bottom here; the pirate cemetery still looks over the Baie des Forbans. By 1730 only a handful of old hands remain, ruling little kingdoms ashore.
population: Dozens of white pirates at the end; their Malagasy families and allies in the thousands
defenses: The reef, the fevers, and the distance from every navy on earth
trade: Plunder fenced for New York rum and gunpowder; slaves traded by the worst of them
events: 1691–97 — Baldridge's post: a pirate general store with harbour fees
1698 — Kidd arrives, scuttles the Adventure Galley; most of his crew defect to Culliford
1720 — England's crew careens the Fancy & Victory; Taylor and La Buse rule the road
1721 — the Cabo brought in; the greatest single prize of the age divided here
1730 — Ratsimilaho, half-English son of a pirate, rules the Betsimisaraka coast as king

name: Mocha
blurb: Coffee port at the Red Sea's mouth; the pilgrim and treasure fleets passing here were the richest prizes ever taken by pirates.
detail: Henry Avery's capture of the Mughal Ganj-i-Sawai off the Bab-el-Mandeb in 1695 — worth perhaps £600,000 — set off the whole 'pirate round'. Every Madagascar pirate dreamed of one Mocha cruise to end them all.
population: ~10,000 in season
defenses: Two small forts; the Imam's goodwill is the real customs house
trade: Coffee — Europe's new addiction — bought with Spanish silver; the Surat pilgrim fleet passes the strait yearly
events: 1695 — Avery takes the Ganj-i-Sawai; Aurangzeb nearly expels the English in fury
1720s — English, French and Dutch factories all buy coffee here

name: Surat
blurb: The Mughal Empire's great western port, dispatching the pilgrim fleet to Jeddah that pirates so loved to meet.
population: ~200,000 — among the great cities of Asia
defenses: The castle commands the Tapti; the Sidi of Janjira's fleet serves the Mughals at sea
trade: Cottons beyond counting, indigo, the hajj fleet's bullion returns
events: 1695 — Avery's outrage: the city mob nearly destroys the English factory in reprisal
1720s — Surat declines as Bombay rises, but its merchants still finance oceans of trade

name: Bombay
blurb: The East India Company's fortified harbour, fighting a running war with the Angrian 'pirate' fleets of the Konkan coast.
detail: Kanhoji Angre's grab-and-gallivat squadrons take Company ships within sight of Bombay through the 1720s. The EIC calls him a pirate; he holds a Maratha commission and an unbeaten record until his death in 1729.
defenses: Bombay Castle, the Marine (the Company's private navy), island geography
trade: Cotton, pepper; shipbuilding in Parsi yards begins its great age
events: 1718 & 1721 — Company expeditions against Angre's forts fail bloodily
1729 — Kanhoji Angre dies undefeated; his sons divide the coast
1735 — master-builder Lowjee Wadia arrives; Bombay teak ships will outlive oak

name: Goa
blurb: Capital of the Estado da Índia, sending the yearly carracks to Lisbon — La Buse took its viceroy's flagship in 1721.
population: ~20,000 and shrinking with the empire
defenses: Aguada fort at the river mouth; processions and prayers
trade: Pepper, the Lisbon carreira, country trade under church bells
events: 1721 — the Nossa Senhora do Cabo, carrying the retiring viceroy and the Goa treasury, falls to Taylor and La Buse at Bourbon — the richest pirate prize ever weighed

name: Madras
blurb: Fort St George, the Company's Coromandel headquarters for cotton cloth and diamonds.
population: ~80,000 in Black Town and White Town
defenses: Fort St George's walls; an open surf roadstead — masula boats or swim
trade: Painted and woven cottons for Europe and the slave trade, Golconda diamonds
events: 1717 — Mughal farman grants the Company trade privileges
1726 — Mayor's Court established; Company law comes ashore

name: Calcutta
blurb: The Company's young Bengal settlement at Fort William, already shipping the subcontinent's richest cargoes.
population: ~100,000 drawn to the flag of trade
defenses: Fort William (old style); the Hooghly's shifting sands as moat and menace
trade: Bengal muslins and silks, saltpetre for Europe's wars, opium beginning
events: 1717 — the great farman exempts Company trade from Bengal customs
1730s — Calcutta passes Madras as the Company's money-spinner

name: Batavia
blurb: The VOC's Asian capital and spice entrepôt, reached by the roaring-forties route through Sunda Strait.
population: ~70,000 within and without the walls — Dutch, Chinese, Javanese, Mardijkers
defenses: The Kasteel and a Dutch-style walled city of canals (and canal fevers)
trade: All the spice monopolies — nutmeg, cloves, cinnamon — plus pepper, tea via China, Japan copper
events: 1722 — Zeewijk, bound here, wrecks on the Abrolhos like the Batavia a century before
1730s — malaria from the new fishponds makes Batavia the 'graveyard of Europeans'
(1740 — the Chinese massacre will stain the city forever)

name: Manila
blurb: Western terminus of the Manila galleon, exchanging American silver for Chinese silk across the world's loneliest sea road.
population: ~40,000: Intramuros Spanish, Parián Chinese, everyone at the galleon's mercy
defenses: Intramuros walls, Fort Santiago; Cavite arsenal across the bay
trade: One or two galleons a year carry it all: Chinese silks and porcelain for Mexican silver
events: 1726 — galleon Santo Cristo de Burgos era's trade limits raised to 300,000 pesos (everyone smuggles triple)
1730s — Chinese junks from Amoy fill the bay each monsoon

name: Canton (Whampoa)
blurb: The single port where the Qing permit European ships; the tea and porcelain fleets anchor at Whampoa reach.
population: ~300,000 and more
defenses: The Bogue forts on the Pearl River; imperial regulation, heavier than stone
trade: Tea (the English habit hardens now), porcelain, silk — for silver only
events: 1720 — the Cohong merchant guild forms to deal with the foreign devils
1730s — a dozen East Indiamen a season load at Whampoa

name: Macau
blurb: Portugal's ancient toehold in China, living politely in the Canton trade's shadow.
defenses: Monte fort; Chinese sufferance
trade: Brokerage, the Japan trade's ghost, opium creeping in
events: 1717 & 1732 — Qing edicts confine and define the foreign trade; Macau survives by being indispensable to it

name: Acapulco
blurb: Pacific port of New Spain; when the galleon arrives from Manila its fair briefly makes Acapulco the richest town in the Americas.
population: ~4,000 between fairs; ~12,000 during one
defenses: Fort San Diego's star walls over the anchorage
trade: The galleon fair: Chinese silks against silver; then ten months of sleep
events: 1715 — fort rebuilt after earthquake
1730s — galleons of 1,000+ tons, the largest ships in the world, load here for Manila

name: Panama City
blurb: Pacific gathering point of Peru's silver before the mule trains cross to Portobelo; rebuilt after Morgan burned it in 1671.
population: ~8,000 in the new walled city
defenses: New site on a rocky point, full walls — Morgan's lesson learned
trade: Peru's silver northbound, pearls from the islands, the South Sea armada
events: 1671 — Morgan burns Old Panama; the city moves and walls itself
1680s–90s — buccaneers cross the isthmus to raid the South Sea; the memory keeps watches manned

name: Callao
blurb: Port of Lima and the South Sea armada, shipping Potosí silver north to Panama.
defenses: City walls and the Armada del Mar del Sur
trade: Silver to Panama, wine, wheat coastwise; French interlopers (until lately) sold Lima its silks
events: 1700s–20s — French Saint-Malo ships dominate an illegal but tolerated trade
1746 — (the great wave will erase it; for now it thrives)

name: Rio de Janeiro
blurb: Outlet of the Minas Gerais goldfields; the gold fleets sail for Lisbon under convoy.
defenses: Santa Cruz fort at the bay mouth — improved since the French embarrassments
trade: Gold, sugar, slaves in from Luanda, the Colônia contraband route to the Plate
events: 1710 — Duclerc's French raid beaten in the streets
1711 — Duguay-Trouin takes and ransoms the city; Portugal builds walls
1720s — gold fleet sails yearly with millions

name: Salvador da Bahia
blurb: Capital of Brazil and hub of the sugar and tobacco fleets; Roberts cut the Sagrada Família out of its convoy in 1719.
population: ~40,000 — the second city of the Portuguese world
defenses: Forts ringing the Bay of All Saints; the fleet system
trade: Sugar, tobacco (the Mina coast's favorite currency), whale oil, slaves in
events: 1719 — Roberts, weeks into piracy, takes the 1719 fleet's richest ship from under 42 sail
1724 — the viceregal capital glitters with Minas gold

name: Recife
blurb: Brazil's easternmost port, where the trade-wind crossing from Africa makes its landfall.
population: ~15,000 with Olinda on its hill
defenses: The natural stone reef that names the town; Brum and Buraco forts
trade: Sugar, brazilwood, Angola slaves' first American port
events: 1710–11 — the 'War of the Mascates' between Recife traders and Olinda planters
1654 — Dutch Brazil ended here, still in living memory

name: Buenos Aires
blurb: Back door of the silver empire: Potosí silver leaks out here through the English asiento and Portuguese Colônia.
defenses: A fort on the bank; mud, distance and the Crown's neglect
trade: Contraband silver and hides for European goods; the South Sea Company's slave asiento factory
events: 1713 — Utrecht gives the English asiento a factory here
1720s — Colônia do Sacramento across the Plate runs the contraband mill

name: St. John's, Newfoundland
blurb: Capital of the cod fishery — and the pirates' recruiting ground: Bartholomew Roberts took 22 ships in this harbour in 1720.
detail: Each summer hundreds of ships fish the Banks and cure cod ashore. The fishing servants, unpaid and brutalized, made eager pirate recruits: when Roberts swept Trepassey and St. John's in June 1720 with black flags flying, men queued to sign his articles.
population: ~3,000 in summer; a tenth winter over
defenses: Small forts at the Narrows, often unmanned off-season
trade: Salt cod to Iberia and the sugar islands; sack ships from the West Country
events: 1720 (June) — Roberts storms Trepassey harbour, 22 prizes; terror of the fishery
1696 & 1708 — French raids burn the town

name: Pondicherry
blurb: The French Compagnie des Indes' Coromandel capital, polite rival to Madras up the coast.
defenses: Fort Louis; Dupleix's ambitions still twenty years off
trade: Painted cottons, the France-bound Indiamen of the reformed Compagnie
events: 1720s — Compagnie des Indes reorganized after Law's bubble; Pondicherry steadies into profit
